data.title: LyondellBasell's Crucial Moment: Will The Board Protect The Dividend?
data.description:
After giving LyondellBasell Industries N.V. (LYB) a Sell rating in October, I think it’s a good time to revisit the company’s outlook following the earnings report released this morning.

Q4 Results

Q4 non‑GAAP EPS came in at –$0.26, missing the $0.25 consensus estimate by a wide margin of $0.51. Revenue was reported at $7.09 billion, down 25.4% year over year, but still beating expectations by $270 million.

Focusing on the weakening margins right away highlights the big challenges the company is dealing with in this tough market environment. Management repeatedly emphasized underlying issues during the earnings call, with CEO Peter Vanacker noting that this is the most severe cycle he has encountered in his career to date.

The way he expressed himself on the earnings release - “...during an exceptionally challenging environment...” in my view, summarizes the situation exceptionally well.

Oxyfuels and Europe PE are currently the only segments performing close to their historical average margins, as shown in the table below from the earnings presentation, while other segments are falling well short of their past figures.

The fourth quarter demonstrated weakness across nearly all areas and segments, with the exception of the technology division, which licenses technology and receives royalties and therefore is not subject to the same operational challenges as other segments. All operational segments reported losses when excluding one-time costs.

The results were especially concerning when considering weakening EBITDA trends. In the most recent quarter, adjusted EBITDA—excluding certain items—was $417 million, representing a decline of over 30% compared to the same period last year. Annualizing these figures suggests EBITDA could fall even below $2 billion if that trend continues, potentially ranging from $1.6 billion to $2 billion if performance does not improve in 2026.

Management noted during the conference call that pricing pressures from China persist due to an oversupplied Chinese market and ongoing efforts by Chinese producers to increase exports. However, they remain cautiously optimistic and anticipate potential signs of rationalization in the future.

Capital Allocation

An analysis of capital allocation indicates that the company's cash balance has remained unchanged primarily due to the issuance of $1.5 billion in bonds. In summary, the company financed its dividend payments and share repurchases largely by incurring additional debt:

Dividends Were Discussed During the Conference Call

During the conference call, CEO Peter Vanacker did not reaffirm as usual the company's commitment to dividends, instead emphasizing the importance of maintaining a strong balance sheet and investment grade rating. He stated that the board is currently reevaluating the dividend and will discuss it at their next meeting in February. After his comments, I observed the share price weaken in real time, likely due to the lack of clear dividend reassurance.

Outlook

Management was most optimistic about the North America segment, where seasonal improvement is anticipated, while other segments are expected to remain stable at best.

During the earnings release and conference call, phrases like "pressure" and "soft demand" came up frequently, in my view highlighting well the ongoing challenges the company is dealing with.

The company didn't share a revenue and profit forecast for 2026, offering only limited information on capital spending, interest expenses, and depreciation and amortization costs.

...and offered some broader insights on market dynamics and operating rates:

Entering the first quarter, the company is managing continued volatility in feedstock and energy prices. In North America, tight year-end inventories, reduced supply due to winter storm Fern and stronger seasonal demand are supportive for polyethylene price increase initiatives in the market. In Europe, typical seasonal trends should lead to improved demand as the quarter unfolds. Oxyfuel profitability is expected to normalize following a volatile 2025 with typical seasonal margin improvements toward the end of the first quarter. LYB is aligning first quarter operating rates with global demand and plans to operate Olefins & Polyolefins Americas assets at approximately 85%, Olefins & Polyolefins EAI assets at approximately 75%, and Intermediates & Derivatives assets at approximately 85%.

Cash Flow, Debt, and Balance Sheet

Although the net debt-to-EBITDA ratio is still under 4, it currently sits at 3.7—satisfying creditors and supporting investment-grade ratings—the financial situation is deteriorating, and without excluding one-time costs, this would have become pretty worrisome.

As previously indicated, their free cash flow does not cover dividend payments, which have lately been financed through the issuance of new debt. Although the fourth quarter free cash flow showed clear improvement over the prior quarter, the annualized free cash flow stayed at about $500 million, falling well short of the roughly $1.9 billion paid out in dividends.

The balance sheet shows a noticeable boost in cash compared to Q3. However, as previously discussed, this is primarily due to the company's issuance of $1.5 billion in new debt, which is reflected in the long-term debt section, rising from $10.5 billion to $12.1 billion year over year.

Valuation

Using the same EV/EBITDA approach from my previous piece in October, I came up with the following results:

Share Price based on EV/EBITDA calculation, billions $

conservative

EV/Ebitda 7

conservative

EV/Ebitda 7

optimistic

EV/Ebitda10

optimistic

EV/Ebitda10
EBITDA 2 3 2 3
Cash 3.4 3.4 3.4 3.4
Debt 12.3 12.3 12.3 12.3
Net Debt 8.9 8.9 8.9 8.9
EV/EBITDA 7 14 21
EV/EBITDA 10 20 30
Equity Value 5.1 12.1 11.1 21.1
Stock count 0.322 0.322 0.322 0.322
Stock Price, Various Scenarios 15.84 37.58 34.47 65.53

Author's Creation.

This time, I modeled four different scenarios. The first two columns show the stock price assuming an EV/EBITDA multiple of 7, which is a typical conservative approach for a company operating in a highly cyclical sector, in my experience. To offer a more optimistic perspective as well, the other two columns show scenarios where the EV/EBITDA multiple is 10, one with EBITDA at $2 billion and the other with EBITDA at $3 billion. As shown in bold, the results vary quite meaningfully. I still think it makes sense to lean toward a mid-range scenario, with the fair share price settling at a reasonable value in the mid-30s for now.

By the relative valuation approach, LYB seems slightly overvalued compared to its 5-year average, but roughly in line with the sector median:

Risks

The company faces a significant feedstock risk: the AI boom is increasing energy demand, which has driven up natural gas prices (NG1:COM). This is crucial, as natural gas is the main pillar of LYB's profitability and competitiveness.

There’s also a considerable political risk: if Chinese authorities view the sector as strategically important and choose to maintain or expand their market share, they could drive prices down over the long term, regardless of economic rationale.

The execution risk of navigating the company through the ongoing, serious downcycle and the length and depth of that cycle is also a significant challenge.

The macro and tariff risks are certainly worth highlighting.

The dividend is at risk. While maintaining the dividend is certainly very important from a shareholder-friendly approach and long-standing traditional standpoint, from the perspective of the company's long-term financial sustainability, it might be better to step back on that front until the business cycle shows clear signs of improvement.

Takeaway

In my opinion, the LYB is still caught in the midst of a really challenging downturn, with no clear end in sight yet. The situation appears to be deteriorating further, as management’s tone has also become more subdued, and there are no bold expectations of significant broad improvement in the near term. The company seems focused on boosting efficiency while keeping its finances sustainable.

Although natural gas prices have previously boosted the company's above-average profitability in the North America region, recent price dynamics don’t provide that advantage at the moment. Furthermore, increased electricity demand from AI and expanding LNG exports seem to be pushing natural gas prices higher over the long term.

Management’s hesitation to directly answer questions about dividends points to a fair amount of uncertainty in that regard, in my view. The decision has been pushed to the board’s next meeting in February, but with finances weakening and the discussion being cautious, I’m not feeling confident about their chances of keeping such a high yield.

In my view, it’s better to stay patient and cautious with LYB until there are clear signs that the cycle is beginning to improve. I don’t see such indications yet.

data.title: Unit Corporation: Cash Position Still Significant After No Special Dividend In 2025
data.description:
Unit Corporation (UNTC) did not declare a special dividend in late 2025 after selling its drilling division. This was a disappointment to some investors, but it does make Unit's current $1.25 per share quarterly dividend sustainable for a longer period of time.

I have updated my valuation estimates for Unit based on end of 2026 projections now. I estimate its end of 2026 value at $29.00 to $33.85 per share if the warrants are not adjusted. The downside scenario would be $26.05 to $30.15 per share if the warrants are fully adjusted for all dividends.

I remain neutral on Unit at its current share price. It does offer a nice 15% yield currently, but may see a bit of a share price decline depending on what happens with the warrants.

Special Dividend And Interest Income

Unit held off on declaring a special dividend after the sale of its drilling division. I had thought there was a decent chance of a special dividend, but that depended on whether or not Unit wanted to maintain a larger cash position to help support its regular dividend for a longer period of time.

The decision not to issue a special dividend leaves Unit with a significant amount of cash on hand. I have been projecting Unit's year-end 2025 cash balance at approximately $180 million, including the proceeds from the sale of its drilling division.

This would translate into approximately $7 million per year in interest income at current rates. Short-term investment rates (such as the one-month T-bill rate) are now around 3.7%, down from around 4.3% to 4.4% in mid-2025. This has a slight effect on Unit's interest income, with a 0.5% to 0.6% change equating to a $1 million difference in annual interest income with its projected year-end 2025 cash balance.

Notes On Hedges

Unit had a fair amount of Q4 2025 natural gas hedges, but its hedges for 2026 are relatively modest.

Unit is approximately 15% hedged on natural gas and 18% hedged on oil in 2026, assuming that it keeps production roughly flat compared to Q3 2025 levels.

This gives Unit significant exposure to fluctuating commodity prices. At current strip prices, Unit's 2026 hedges have roughly neutral value. Unit's natural gas hedges have slightly negative value, while its oil hedges have slightly positive value.

Updated 2026 Outlook

Since I last looked at Unit, commodity strip prices for 2026 have improved somewhat. The current 2026 strip is now around $63 to $64 for WTI oil (CL1:COM) and around $4.60 for NYMEX natural gas (NG1:COM).

Natural gas prices have soared due to the winter storms, with February's contract closing at $7.46. The effect is smaller on later contracts, with the March to June 2026 contracts averaging slightly over $4.

The overall effect of the winter storms on Unit's projected 2026 free cash flow is to improve it by a bit over $10 million compared to mid-January strip prices, but the improvement is only a few million compared to when I looked at Unit in November 2025 (since natural gas futures were relatively strong then).

I am continuing to model Unit's 2026 production at roughly Q3 2025 levels, although this will depend on Unit's participation in non-operated activity.

At current strip prices, I now project Unit to generate $115 million in revenues.

Type Barrels/Mcf Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 800,000 $61.50 $49
NGLs 1,000,000 $16.25 $16
Natural Gas 12,000,000 $3.70 $44
Interest Income $7
Hedge Value $0
Total $115

This results in a projection of $36 million in free cash flow in 2026, assuming $20 million in capex to hold production roughly flat. Unit's cash G&A post-drilling divestiture remains an unknown, although I've modeled that at $14 million per year.

$ Million
Oil and Gas Operating Costs $45
Cash G&A $14
Capital Expenditures $20
Total $79

This would result in roughly 0.7x dividend coverage at a $1.25 per share quarterly dividend. Unit's projected cash balance would decrease by $13 million to $167 million at the end of 2026 in this scenario.

Unit would have approximately 1.0x dividend coverage (at $1.25 per share per quarter) at around $75 WTI oil and $4.50 NYMEX natural gas (without hedges). This also assumes $7 million per year in interest income.

Notes On Valuation

I have updated my valuation estimates for Unit for the end of 2026 projections now. I have assumed that Unit will end 2026 with $167 million in cash.

I have also assumed that it mostly replenishes its PD reserves during 2026, resulting in an upstream value of $120 million to $168 million at the end of 2026.

This would result in an estimated value of $29.00 to $33.85 per share at the end of 2026 for Unit, based on 9.9 million outstanding shares. This assumes no impact from the warrant litigation.

A scenario where the warrants end up being fully adjusted for all dividends would reduce Unit's estimated value to $26.05 to $30.15 per share. Unit's warrants would have an exercise price of $9.24 at the end of 2026 in this scenario.

A scenario where the warrants were adjusted only for the $32 per share in special dividends paid out would result in minimal impact to Unit's estimated value. In that scenario, I'd estimate a $29.00 to $33.50 per share valuation for Unit.

Unit's warrants could be worth approximately $17 to $21 at the end of 2026 if they are fully adjusted for all dividends, but would probably only be worth a couple of dollars if the adjustment was only for special dividends and nothing if there ended up being no adjustments.

Conclusion

Unit has a significant amount of cash on hand now, which boosts its interest income. After selling its drilling division, its dividend coverage is likely to be below 1.0x, but it has enough cash on hand to support its current $1.25 per share dividend for quite a long time.

I currently project Unit to end up with $13 million in cash burn after dividends in 2026, which would reduce its cash position by around 7%. I am neutral on Unit at its current share price of $33, although it may have attractiveness to investors prioritizing dividend income (it should be noted that the tax treatment of its dividends has varied over time).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Momentum Regime Is Changing - And It Favors XSMO Over SPMO
data.description:
In June 2025, I had described the Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO) as a tactical momentum vehicle rather than a buy-and-forget allocation. The core of the thesis was that SPMO excels in specific market regimes. It needs narrow leadership to lead a momentum charge and persistent trends to work out well. SPMO thrives when leadership trends for months at a time but struggles to deliver alpha over the broad market index (SPY) during consolidation, dispersions, and rotations/reversals.

In June, I had felt that the strong market for narrow leadership in large caps, where momentum did matter across the board, had largely played out. What I expected ahead was slowing momentum and dispersion within the large- and mega-cap universes. The momentum continued to remain strong for a while, but dispersion started to creep in. For a heavily concentrated style like SPMO, that often means it relies on being on the right side of concentration in such an environment of dispersion. Since my June Hold call on SPMO, SPY is up by ~16% while SPMO has delivered only ~11% in total return terms. So that June call has played out as expected.

Interestingly, while dispersion is not a necessary structural tailwind for the mega-cap dominated large cap universe, it remains so for the small cap universe for several reasons. One, dispersion here is generally higher. There are stocks that are riding momentum even when broader, smaller cap indices are consolidating. Stock selection on the basis of momentum matters more in the small cap universe always and adds the potential to generate alpha in the long run. That is where momentum strategies now start rewarding the Invesco S&P SmallCap Momentum ETF (XSMO), where dispersion is a relatively more persistent theme and concentration is more balanced to be able to generate momentum tailwinds even if the markets are not rallying at the index level. Interestingly, XSMO is also almost as close to a buy and forget allocation as can be within the momentum ETF universe, where SPMO is often a tactical bet.

Consistent Edge in XSMO - Performance Data

XSMO has delivered persistent long term alpha vs. IWM. This is seen in and around the COVID crash and subsequent rebound and through the 2022 weakness (a regime when several momentum plays generally are expected to give back alpha over their broader parent). This ability to hold on to alpha in weaker markets does not rob XSMO of the ability to grab alpha in rallies (like that seen in 2024-25).

XSMO's sustained alpha generation is in contrast with SPMO, where SPMO needs conducive momentum-driven bull legs to outperform. It gives back much of its gains in momentum crashes or even sideways markets. The alpha generation abilities are often overlooked when we compare total returns of XSMO versus SPMO. SPMO is far ahead in total return terms but requires more work from the broader market than its own alpha generating abilities. That matters when supportive regimes turn.

XSMO's Structural Edge

XSMO's consistent alpha generation - a result of holding back gains when the broader small cap index is not showing any momentum of its own and amplifying it when momentum takes center stage - is not a result of any methodological edge as such.

Both SPMO and XSMO use nearly identical definitions of momentum—12-month price return excluding the most recent month, adjusted for volatility to favor more stable trends. Stocks are ranked by a risk-adjusted momentum score, weighted by a blend of market cap and momentum score, and rebalanced semi-annually.

The structural edge comes from the universe both ETFs apply these rules to. SPMO selects ~100 stocks from a universe of 500 stocks (the S&P 500). These stocks are heavily owned. Price moves are more macro- and sector driven and correlation (even during a phase of dispersion) is usually higher. Market caps here tend to skew toward mega caps in a steeper way, and concentration builds up. XSMO, on the other hand, draws from a slightly larger universe of the S&P SmallCap 600 (and a far larger universe of small caps by number overall - say 2000 in the case of the Russell 2000 small cap index). Here, stock selection usually has material significance because of the larger universe, plus greater performance dispersion anyway due to lower analyst consensus and crowding, wider earnings variability, and greater stock specific price discovery (often greater but more volatile momentum too). The market cap dominance does not show up at the same level of concentration as it does in SPMO too. The top 10 holdings here account for only ~26% of the portfolio compared to ~51% concentration in the case of SPMO.

This diversification is also visible in sectoral allocations. Unlike SPMO, where nearly ~70% of exposure sits in three mega-cap-driven sectors (tech, communications, and financials), XSMO’s leadership is spread across cyclicals, industrials, and smaller growth segments, with no single sector dominating the portfolio. The exact sectoral allocation matters for total returns (such as higher tech allocations in SPMO), but not for alpha generation. That assessment is important for an SPY versus IWM thesis, not for momentum investors.

What matters for a momentum strategy generating alpha over the broader index is how well diversified its exposure is. That indicates whether it is deriving performance from a dominant theme or relying on company specific momentum and growth (not broad macro flows into a specific sector). XSMO's sectoral allocation is more incidental to stock selection. In the case of SPMO, right now it seems to be the opposite case.

The overall implications of the portfolio structure, methodology, and the universes the ETFs cater to are that SPMO requires its concentrated bets to go right. That happens when we have a 2024-25 like market where the momentum is stronger in the mega cap narrow leadership. Any dispersion in the mega cap bucket implies a drag. XSMO does not require any concentrated theme to play out. It relies on stock-specific momentum to work out across the portfolio. It can do well even if small caps are not trending up as a group. Dispersion is not a drag for XSMO.

XSMO for All Regimes, Especially Now

All in all, SPMO remains a Hold because it is expected to underperform SPY in the next few quarters until broader growth returns in the mega cap pack. Even in comparison with XSMO, its long term thesis is not superior anyway in terms of thematic purity - that is, momentum as a source of consistent alpha. And that is a structural issue rather than something governed by short term dynamics. For the same structural superiority, XSMO is a better long-term Buy for momentum investors.

In total return terms, SPMO benefits from its concentration in favorable conditions - the once-in-a-decade boost it got in 2024-25. That is not usual performance across cycles. In fact, XSMO's outperformance is already visible as SPMO's specific concentrated structure has started to lose its edge recently. The comparison between SPMO and XSMO is not only about total returns, though. If the large cap base is helping SPMO, then SPY remains a better bet. The same cannot be said of XSMO, where risk-adjusted returns are clearly superior to the broader underlying small cap trends.

Looking ahead, let us understand why XSMO is better placed than SPMO in the short term too. On the surface, large caps look better placed than small caps because the latter need a phase of economic expansion, lower rates, and higher liquidity to flourish. That is not on the horizon, not in the next few months at least. So, the small caps' relative underperformance of over a decade is unlikely to reverse soon, and that being a support for XSMO is ruled out. Additionally, as momentum slows, I feel investors will seek refuge in cash backed large and mega caps over narratives alone or smaller caps. That phenomenon has already been in progress over the past few quarters or so, when several narrative stock valuations have been challenged. Mega cap tech hasn't really taken off, but either remains defensive or is still moving up, although slower than what we saw in 2024-25.

Despite the relatively bullish view on large caps, SPMO's thesis suffers. This is because SPMO needs a lot more than relative resilience. It needs strong momentum, and that too without dispersion in the narrow leadership it bets on. XSMO does not need strong momentum at the broader small cap index level. It simply needs a market that is not under pressure. As long as there is scope for a few stocks' earnings to expand, XSMO will actually use dispersion to its benefit and ride selective momentum better - both because of its lack of concentration drag and a universe where momentum is usually stronger as a theme (although admittedly volatile, some stocks can go through strong trends due to lack of crowding). Today's environment is therefore conducive to outperformance in XSMO, without relying on small caps mean reverting after years of underperformance. That bounce back may come eventually too for longer term investors but is an added support, not a requirement for today's thesis.

data.title: HomeStreet Acquisition Already Paying Dividends For Mechanics Bancorp
data.description:
This analysis serves as an update to my last article about Mechanics Bancorp (MCHB) that was written in November. Earlier this morning, the company released its financial results from Q4 2025, the first quarterly update since it completed a reverse merger with HomeStreet Bank on September 2. I thought it was worth checking back in to get a better idea of the impact of this recent transaction.

Q4 GAAP earnings per share of $0.54 for MCHB were much better than the expected $0.22. However, the large upside surprise can be explained by a non-recurring transaction. In December, the company announced that it was selling a segment of the assets that had been acquired in its merger with HomeStreet. Once this deal was finalized, Mechanics was able to record a bargain purchase gain of $55.1 million, which represented 44.3% of the total net income from Q4.

Even when this temporary boost is taken out of the equation, there is still a lot to like about the recent performance of the company. In my first analysis, I saw MCHB as a Hold. I am upgrading that opinion to a Buy based on the prospects for the company in the year ahead.

Company Overview

Mechanics Bancorp is the holding company for Mechanics Bank, which is headquartered just outside of San Francisco, California. The bank has 166 branches with the large majority of those located in the company’s home state. Mechanics has a cluster of branches along the Pacific Coast of Washington State, four in the metro area around Portland, Oregon, and four in the state of Hawaii. The bank is now the third largest in California by deposit market share and the fourth largest on the West Coast. A complete map of the company’s service area along with additional details about its operations can be seen below.

At the end of 2025, Mechanics Bancorp reported total assets of $22.4 billion, a decline of 1.6% since the end of Q3. The decrease in assets is due to the reduction in the bank’s loan portfolio which had a value of $14.2 billion, about $400 less than at the end of the previous quarter. MCHB also held a securities' portfolio worth just over $5.3 billion as of December 31. For a more detailed discussion of those securities assets, readers are encouraged to look back at my previous article.

Deposits at the bank were valued at $19.0 billion at the end of the last quarter. Those balances fell $428 million when compared to Q3, but the decline was caused by run-off of $603 million from time deposits that were paying higher levels of interest. The company did see an increase of $210 in interest-bearing demand and money market balances. Over a third of the deposits at Mechanics Bank, 35.4% to be exact, are held in non-interest bearing accounts. The total cost of deposits for the bank was 1.43% for Q4 and 1.40% for FY 2025. This is well below the national average for a bank of its size.

The company provided additional detail for its loan mix which is shown below. Commercial real estate takes up a significant share of the portfolio with multifamily CRE alone representing 37.6% of the total. Investment CRE and owner-occupied CRE have weights of 12.3% and 4.9%, respectively, bringing the total CRE allocation over 50%. Residential loans also makeup a sizeable share of the total loans from the bank at 28.0%. The bank is in the process of eliminating its auto loans, and that segment has been in consistent decline throughout the last year.

After not paying a dividend in 2024, Mechanics Bancorp initiated a quarterly distribution in December of 2025. The amount of $0.21 gives the stock an impressive forward yield of 5.6%. The company said that it was able to start the dividend following the HomeStreet merger because its regulatory capital ratios were “stronger than initially anticipated.” As part of its Q4 earnings presentation, the company stated that it expected its Q1 2026 dividend to be an even larger $0.39. If maintained throughout the year, that would give the stock a forward yield of over 10%.

Analysis Of Mechanics Bancorp

The company reported $124.3 million in net income in Q4 2025, far above the $55.2 million reported in Q3. However, that number included the asset sale mentioned earlier. Even when all unusual items are removed, MCHB recorded $59.8 million in core net income, an improvement of 15.9% when compared to Q4 2024.

Many of the other metrics for the bank were impacted by one-time events. The reported Q4 return on assets of 2.2% and return on tangible common equity of 28.5% are not sustainable. However, the bank did report core ROA of 1.06% with the projection that it would rise to 1.40% by 2027. The efficiency ratio from the bank fell from 62.3% to 46.9% in just three months, and that number will likely be higher in Q1 2026. However, MCHB also projects its efficiency ratio will fall below 50% again by the end of 2026.

Net interest margin rose 9 basis points in the last quarter to 3.47%. This number is slightly below average for a bank’s of Mechanic’s size, but there is reason to believe it will continue to trend higher. The bank is deploying cash on hand into higher earning securities and benefiting from continued run-off from time deposits. The average loan yield for the bank increased from 5.13% at the end of Q3 to 5.30% at the end of Q4.

The valuation for the stock is the only thing that is keeping me from being more optimistic about its prospects. MCHB currently trades at a PE ratio of 16.78 for the last 12 months, well above the median for its sector. However, the analysts’ expectation for the company is a profit of $1.24 in FY 2026, which would bring its P/E closer to a much more reasonable 12. A part of its Q3 report, the company said it was on track to deliver approximately $82 million in cost savings by the end of Q2 2026 and expected to earn $302 million in FY 2026. If it hits that mark, that would give MCHB an annual EPS of about $1.46 per share, translating into a PE ratio of 10.3 at the current share price.

The company’s tangible book value per share stands at $7.81, which means the stock is trading at 1.92x its TBV at its current price of $14.92. That is a higher multiple that I like to see in a regional bank. However, that TBV is an increase of 16.6% from the end of 2024, showing that it is expanding at a high rate.

Asset Quality

The asset quality for the loans from Mechanics Bank appear to be strong, and the bank seems prepared for future losses with good reserve coverage. Up until the previous quarter, most of the bank’s charge offs were related to its auto loans which are in a state of run-off. Net charge offs did increase in Q4 but were still lower than at the end of 2024. Excluding auto loans, non-performing assets declined in Q4 from 0.26% to 0.21%. Allowance for credit losses declined eight basis points from the previous quarter to 1.08% at the end of 2025. The ratio of delinquent loans to total loans stood at 0.66% at the end of 2025, compared to 1.01% 12 months prior.

Risks To Consider

While Mechanics Bancorp has stated that it is trying to reduce its dependence on commercial real estate and create greater diversity in its loan portfolio, the process of doing so will be a slow one. According to the company’s Q4 conference call, the bank’s CRE concentration ratio decreased 16 basis points from Q3 to 344% in Q4 2025. Management stated that it expects CRE concentration to eventually drop below 300%.

Nonetheless, the current loans in this category represent a significant amount of risk for the bank, especially if the market rate for rents declines in the year ahead. The bank has a significant exposure to both office and retail CRE which have both been facing market headwinds, although office CRE has seen a comeback in some major cities. Almost a third of the bank’s CRE is based in Los Angeles which also poses added risk as these loans depend on issues that may be unique to the economy of that one city.

The Ford Financial Fund owns approximately 74% of Mechanics Bancorp. Ford Financial is a private equity firm that gained control of MCHB in 2015. This could be seen as a positive for Mechanics shareholders as the firm does have a good track record of success with its control of other regional banks, but it also means that other common shareholders have little say in the operations or leadership of the company.

Mechanics Bancorp has signaled that it may engage in additional M&A activity in the near future. This could expose the bank to greater risks that were taken on by another management team that may not have the same lending standards as MCHB. Depending on the terms of the assets that are acquired, additional transactions could dilute current shareholders and drive a wider gap between the stock’s price and its tangible book value. There is already a significant disparity between MCHB's book value, which was $12.93 at the end of Q4, and its tangible book value of $7.81.

The Bottom Line

The biggest concerns that I have for MCHB going forward is its slightly elevated current valuation and its significant dependence on the state of California for its overall health. The economic outlook for the Golden State is weaker than many others because of its high tax rates and regulation. Nonetheless, the operations of Mechanics Bank look strong and management's positive outlook for the future from certainly takes the business climate in which it operates into consideration.

My first rating of MCHB has a Hold was partially based on a lack of clarity on just what the future for the company would look like post-merger. There has been several one-time events that have impacted the company’s bottom line in both Q3 and Q4 2025. However, I believe it is now a little easier to see what the future for the bank may hold. The attractive yield is also a part of my change in opinion. I have decided to upgrade Mechanics Bancorp to a Buy as it feel it will outperform others in its peer group in the year ahead.

data.title: Silicom Is Earning Its Revenue Back But Profitability Seems Far Away
data.description:
Silicom Ltd. (SILC) reported Q4 25 earnings yesterday, showing continued revenue gains on the back of design wins during the year. The company is also expecting double-digit revenue growth in 2026, with Q1 starting in the high teens.

Albeit this continues to represent a healthy development for the company, I believe its path to profitability remains long, at least at current speeds. Based on simple napkin math, the company should grow revenues at 50% just to break even, which, even at 20% clips, should take 2/3 years, a period during which the inventory cycle in semis might turn negative again.

The name remains a Hold from a fundamental long-term basis, but given its large cash holdings, it also remains an interesting speculative play on a faster-than-expected recovery.

Revenue Gains, Design Wins, and Expectations

As reported in my initial article on SILC, the company is slowly climbing out of a revenue decline caused by downstream destocking and the probable obsolescence of some of its revenue streams.

For FY25, growth has been accelerating on the back of a total of 8 design wins. The whole year closed at ~7% revenue growth, whereas Q4 posted 17% growth, and Q1 is expected to reach 17% as well (earnings call).

In my previous article, I speculated that each of SILC's design wins seems to add about $2 million in yearly revenue. Recent wins in late 2025 show a potentially better picture, with $5 million, $3 million, and $2 million wins. Given that the press releases talk of some of that revenue flowing through 2026, it seems that the contracts will take some time to ramp up. Additionally, we did not get any information on the release or the call (the 20-F has not been published yet) about obsolescence or maturity in other contracts offsetting these wins.

The company expects to repeat the feat in 2026, obtaining around 7/9 new design wins and delivering double-digit growth (unclear if closer to low-teens or high-teens).

Slight Margin Leverage

The company was able to leverage gross margins by 1 pp in Q4 and about 1.3 pp in FY25. This is important for future profitability and reasonable from a manufacturing structure perspective (higher utilization).

On OpEx, leverage was also a reality, with the figure growing ~1% in Q4 (vs. 17% for revenues) and 4.3% for the year (vs. 7% for revenues).

Continued earnings leverage is a key driver in the model I propose below.

Moonshot Markets

The company dedicated part of the release, the call, and the most recent investor presentation to mention three "blue ocean" markets where the company could grow faster than it is growing on its "core offer" today.

These are AI inference (which requires more edge networking, an area of specialization for Silicom), post-quantum cryptography (Silicom offers specialized hardware accelerators), and white-label switching equipment (the company already offers other white-label equipment).

During the call, management conceded that these markets will not represent a big part of growth within the near term; however, despite the grandiose dozen billion TAM they sport in the investor presentation.

The Path to Profits

The situation for Silicom has not changed that much in the past few months since my last coverage.

The company maintains a sturdy balance sheet, which represents its main attractiveness as a speculative play: $74 million in cash and securities and $52 million in inventories, against $35 million in total liabilities and a market cap of $110 million. That is, the company trades barely above its liquidation value.

The losses have decreased, but not that significantly, compared to 2024. For the year, operating losses were $12.3 million versus $13.3 million, and for the quarter, $2.8 million versus $3.7 million. The company makes very little out of its securities, less than $1.5 million a year (effective rate of 2% on its $74 million in holdings), meaning most losses eat into cash.

At this speed, the company will take a long time to reach breakeven, even if growing at 17% forward. For example, assuming the company reaches a gross margin level of 32% (2/3 pp above current levels) and that OpEX remains absolutely fixed (not the case this year), revenues should still grow to $100 million to cover $32 million in OpEx. From $62 million for the year, or $68 million annualized from Q4 levels, it would take between 2.5 and 3 years to reach breakeven. And that is assuming 17% growth continuously, gross margin leverage, and OpEx fixed for three years, and across a 60% aggregate growth in revenues.

Without profitability and without a plan to return the cash to shareholders, the company is basically a slowly decreasing pile of funds. It is growing, but even at current speeds, it will take a fairly long time for the company to at least keep its cash.

For this reason, I believe Silicom remains a Hold. Still, the name remains interesting as a speculative play because in case profitability improves even moderately for a few quarters, the stock can move fairly violently on a rerating of expectations. A recent example of such a move was my call on 1stdibs.com (DIBS).

data.title: Knight-Swift Transportation Trades At A Premium Of Already Optimistic Assumptions
data.description:
Knight-Swift Transportation (KNX) is a US truckload, less than truckload, and intermodal carrier, one of the largest in the US public markets by overall tractors, and the largest in one-way truckload.

The company sported some fairly high and resilient returns before the post-pandemic downturn in trucking, which we currently experience. The combination of an extended and vicious downturn with acquisitions and additional leverage has depressed net income to levels not seen in more than a decade.

Although the company seems attractive as a vehicle for speculating on a turnaround of the cycle, I believe it is already priced not only for a recovery but also for even more. Even using figures that exclude the recent downturn, earnings yields only reach mid-single-digit (MSD) figures, which I believe are unacceptable for a trucking company. I initiate with a Hold.

Largest Truckload One-Way in the US

Knight-Swift has a long history going back to the 1960s, but the company as we know it today is the result of the merger of Knight and Swift in 2017.

In its aggregate size, of around 27 thousand tractors in total, the company stands as one of the largest fleets, close to names like JB Hunt (JBHT) or Schneider (SNDR).

Different from this, however, is the company's focus on one-way, for-hire shipments. The company's main business throughout most of the past decade, and even today still ~65% of revenues (FY24 Annual Report), is truckload. Within this, the company informs us in its 4Q25 results presentation (page 5) that about 70% is for-hire, one-way truckload. The remaining 30% is dedicated (that is, managed for a single shipper). This makes the company, according to its annual report, the largest one-way truckload fleet in the country. Other companies have similar fleets, but they operate in more contracted, long-term segments, like dedicated or intermodal.

As commented in other articles covering trucking names, one-way is the most competitive and volatile of the trucking sub-segments. The spot market is the first to react to both oversupply and undersupply. Considering that, the fact that the company had been able to earn a 13% return on tangible assets until 2021 is fairly interesting by itself.

The company recognizes the challenges in this market but believes it will provide the most upside leverage when capacity becomes tight. Quoting from its annual report: "This same one-way capacity that was the most commoditized service in the over-supplied market becomes the most valuable offering to our customers in a tighter market".

LTL Expansion

In 2021, the company acquired AAA Cooper Transportation, a national operator in the less-than-truckload (LTL) market. In late 2021, it acquired another LTL carrier, MME. Finally, in 2024, the company acquired a third provider, Dependable Highway Express.

The goal of the company has been to enter and expand in the LTL market. This market is already a mix of trucking, warehousing, and networks because it requires a large network of hubs where the carrier reaccommodates shipments that originally cannot fill a whole truck (that is why it is called LTL).

Some of the companies with the best operating returns on assets in the trucking industry, in particular, Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL), are in LTL. It is a market considered less commoditized and more protected.

Unfortunately, we cannot know the exact returns on KNX's LTL segment in a regular cycle because operations started in earnest in 2022 (when the cycle was already turning) and because the company does not report assets by segment (a very relevant data point to establish the segment's capital returns).

What we can know is that the operating margins in LTL are not particularly high today: 7% in FY24, 11% in FY23, and 12% in FY22 (all from the Annual Report in 2024). The margin in 4Q25 was 5%. Compare that with more specialized LTL competitors, even in the current cycle. This is not the best operation in the industry.

Leverage is Manageable

In order to do the LTL acquisitions, the company had to add debt. Coinciding with a downturn in trucking, this led to a significant leveraging in terms of Net Debt/EBITDA and also for interest to get closer to operating income.

Of course, as seen above, EBITDA is way above interest, and therefore, the company could invest less than D&A in order to cover interest if operating income fell even more. This, however, eventually reaches a limit in terms of how much the asset base can depreciate before efficiencies decrease (trucks really depreciate quickly as they are used).

Valuation is Beyond Optimistic

My main concern when looking at KNX is that the company's return on assets had dramatically changed after the acquisition of the LTL segment because it is operating in a different industry, in which it may lack execution and competitive advantages. If that is the case, then the long-term return might not look like the past (for example, the average in future cycles might not be 13% but less than 10%).

However, the price of KNX already dispels that risk because it embeds the expectation that KNX's returns will be 50% or 100% higher than they were before the pandemic, making this stock an easy pass.

Assuming, very optimistically, that trucking conditions in 2022-2025 are not representative of new competitive challenges in the industry, but rather just a blip in its history; and that KNX's historical asset returns will belong to their median returns before the acquisition of the LTL companies, even though this segment now represents about 20% of revenues; we would expect KNX to return to generate about $870 million in operating income in a few years (13% return on $6.7 billion in tangible assets).

This kind of operating income is already 170%+ above the current TTM levels and close to historical peaks in 2021/22, but now with more assets. This should make the stock increase in price, albeit I am concerned that the current stock price already discounts this scenario, and then some more.

From those $870 million, we should remove about $150 million in net interest and 25% in taxes to get to $540 million in net income. That is the net income to be expected across a cycle if we assume that the 2022-2025 cycle was abnormal, non-repeatable and that the company's current asset configuration will earn the return it did before the pandemic, even though it now participates in a different industry (where it performs below peers).

Compared to that, the company's current market cap is already $9.4 billion, or almost 17.5x the normalized, optimistic earnings I calculated above. This represents an earnings yield of 5.7%, well below the historical return of the market.

And this is with an optimistic view of the company's long-term returns on assets. If we decreased them to 10% alone, operating income would fall to $670 million, net income to $390 million, and the earnings yield to 4% alone.

I believe that, in the current context, and based on the performance of KNX's segments after the pandemic, paying a premium on an optimistic outlook makes little sense. For that reason, I initiate KNX with a Hold rating.

That said, for readers who interpret my thesis with a bearish tone, some upside risks are important. The trucking cycle has been long and harsh, and if demand for trucking increases rapidly (for example, because of a review of tariff policy), rates could turn positive very fast. KNX is especially leveraged to the trucking cycle because of its exposure to the for-hire market. In such a case, with earnings growing rapidly, the stock could get positively rerated way above what I would consider the justifiable fundamentals, even compared to today's standards.

data.title: Tesla After Q4 Earnings: Autonomy Takes Over Fundamentals, Again
data.description:
I rated Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) as a hold in my earnings preview piece. I expected fundamentals to deteriorate, but I argued that the autonomy story would steal the show anyway.

I believe I was mostly right, as Q4 fundamentals were raw, although the consensus was slightly more pessimistic than the print. For the first time since Q3 2024, non-GAAP EPS came in higher than the Street's expectations (50 cents vs 45 cents consensus). That said, auto revenue was down 11% YOY, total revenue down 3% YOY, both GAAP net income and GAAP EPS were down sharply YOY, and free cash flow experienced a 30% YOY haircut.

Yet, this deterioration didn't seem to matter from a share price perspective, as CEO Elon Musk and the management team didn't fail to impress the market with the updates on the autonomy story.

The push on autonomy after the Q4 print is even stronger than last year. The company is discontinuing the production of the Model S and X to make space for the Optimus production line.

On robotaxis, Elon reiterated the April timeline to start Cybercab production, and the last earnings report contains a table with several metro areas and the expected rollout timeline, mostly H1 2026. Even though I'm very skeptic on these dates (consider that the earlier goal to reach 8-10 metro areas by the end of 2025 was largely missed), I believe Tesla has a good chance to expand fully autonomous rides beyond Austin this year, and the lowest hanging fruit seems to be the Bay Area, where Waymo has been reported to operate around 1,000 robotaxis.

This update is mainly focused on the Q4 print, and one of the risks with the robotaxi strategy (related to the Cybercab) that I believe is worth monitoring this year.

Before I go into the details, a quick clarification on why I say that the shares were up after earnings. The broader markets experienced a sharp risk-off move on Thursday, pressuring Tesla. Prior to the first regular hour of January 29 (when the SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) sold off by around 0.8%), Tesla was actually up low single digits. That's why I say that shares were up after earnings. The selloff on Thursday was mainly driven by beta, and not by cracks in the autonomy story, even though the CapEx guide was quite high, in my view.

Q4 Highlights

I think the quarter supports the long-term narrative shift from a mundane EV unit growth story to a physical AI platform, revolving around autonomy (robotaxis + FSD), robotics (Optimus), and AI infrastructure (Cortex 2 buildout and AI5&6 inference chips).

As I said in my pre-earnings coverage, this shift in narrative is the key pillar of the bull case on Tesla, and, in my view, it is how one should value this company. That's why I believe that the talk matters more than the fundamentals.

That said, fundamentals weren't as bad as I initially anticipated. In fact, the company beat the EPS consensus by 5 cents, delivering 50 cents in earnings during the fourth quarter. For reference, the last time Tesla beat the EPS consensus was back in Q3 2024.

In my view, the driver was the outperformance of the energy storage business. Tesla delivered a record Q4 storage deployments (14.2 GWh), totaling 46.7 GWh for FY25. Q4 revenue in this segment was up 25% YOY and 27% YOY for the full year. In comparison, the automotive business reported a 10% YOY decline in sales in Q4.

Looking at the bottom line, the company reported for the fifth consecutive quarter a record energy storage gross profit ($1.1B in Q4, up 42%). Interestingly enough, the automotive segment reported a 20% YOY due to the decrease in deliveries year over year and lower average cost per unit due to sales mix and lower material costs, according to the 10-K.

Moving on to the robotaxi segment, Elon busted the concerns that some of the driverless rides in Austin had a chase car behind them during the call.

And we're able to do our first rides with no safety monitor in the car in Austin. These are paid rides. So these are just sort of randomly selected paid rides with no safety monitor. And I think maybe -- as of maybe yesterday or so, we actually don't -- we don't even have a chase car or anything like that. So these are just cars with no people in them and no one is following the car in Austin.

From a deployment perspective, the company published a planned coverage expansion list.

As I anticipated in my earnings preview, the company largely missed the target to scale robotaxi operations in 8-10 metro areas by year-end 2025, particularly in Nevada, Florida, and Arizona (which were specifically mentioned in Q3). Did that matter for the stock? Not really. Targets slipping into later dates are a classic for Tesla (i.e., they are mostly priced in before the delay is officially announced).

Most importantly for the bull case is the fact that the Robotaxi iOS app no longer has a waitlist, which suggests that a ramp is ahead. Unfortunately, Elon didn't disclose the actual number of vehicles deployed in Austin or the Bay Area. However, some sources estimate a total fleet of 255 vehicles across the Bay Area (175) and Austin (80).

In comparison, Alphabet's (GOOG) Waymo has a fleet of approximately 1,000 vehicles in the Bay Area alone.

Moving on, the next highlight was the growth in active FSD subscriptions (up 38% YOY). Even though the company recently switched to a fully subscription-based model, eliminating the one-time purchase option, this seems to be a headwind for automotive margins in the near term:

FSD adoption continued to improve in the quarter, reaching nearly 1.1 million paid customers globally. Of these, nearly 70% were upfront purchases. It is important to note that beginning this quarter, we are transitioning fully to a subscription-based model for FSD. Therefore, net additions to this figure will primarily be via subscription model and in the short term will impact automotive margins.

Finally, on humanoids, the company plans to unveil the Gen 3 Optimus in Q1 2026. At the moment, this program is still in the R&D phase, and volume production is not expected until year-end, with an eventual planned capacity of 1M robots per year (I wouldn't hold my breath until then). A good part of the CapEx ramp this year is to support robotics and the related manufacturing lines, particularly in Fremont. This brings me to the next section.

Risks

Management framed 2026 as a very big CapEx year: +$20B big to be precise. To put this figure into perspective, FY25 CapEx was around $8.5B.

Part of that investment will be used to expand the data center capacity. The main focus is on the Cortex 2 complex in Texas, with a goal to more than double AI training compute in the first half of the year.

On top of that, the development of AI5 and AI6 chips for inference is another area that will require investment. I already discussed the importance of this strategy in an earlier piece. However, I don't see any upside for 2026, after management reiterated that production for these chips is planned for 2027 and 2028. I expected a pull forward into 2026 for the AI5, given the 50x performance improvement vs the AI4.

Even though Q4 gross margin was the highest in over two years (20.1%), free cash flow fell 30% YOY to $1.420B. Bear in mind that the CapEx spend last year came in below the $9B guided in Q3. Overall, I expect to see FCF pressure this year due to the hike in CapEx, although I think the impact on the share price will be limited, as I strongly believe the narrative prevails over the fundamentals of this company.

That said, any cracks in the autonomy story could hurt the stock.

The main risk that I see here is for Cybercab. Contrary to federal design standards, this vehicle doesn't have pedals or a steering wheel.

Back in June 2025, the NHTSA announced it would streamline Part 555 exemptions, which include vehicles without steering wheels, pedals, and mirrors. Since then, Amazon's (AMZN) Zoox received an exemption from the NHTSA.

However, there is a caveat worth considering:

The exemption will continue to allow manufacturers to sell up to 2,500 motor vehicles per year that do not fully comply with the Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standards. This includes vehicles that do not have traditional steering wheels, driver-operated brakes, or rearview mirrors.

The 2,500 cap is bad news from a robotaxi scaling perspective. Therefore, I recommend keeping a close eye on any revisions to Part 555 exemptions from the NHTSA. In the long term, this cap could mean the Cybercab may need to add a steering wheel and pedals, just to avoid being limited to 2,500 units a year.

According to Elon, there are no design changes related to the steering wheel and pedals for the April production models:

But the Cybercab, which is a specific vehicle model that we're making, does not have a steering wheel or pedals. So this is clearly -- there's no fallback mechanism here. It's like this car either drives itself or it does not drive. And we expect to start production in April.

My concern is that this could change later this year, which could hurt the stock, given that adding a steering wheel and pedals could limit the number of passengers from 2 to 1.

Conclusion

As I hinted in my pre-earnings preview, despite some of the disappointments in Q4, the stock inched higher after the earnings report. To be honest, my biggest disappointment was the delay in the robotaxi rollout. Elon guided 8–10 areas before the end of 2025. I was expecting, at the very least, one or two new metro areas added in Q4, not zero.

Looking ahead, I will be monitoring whether Tesla closes the gap versus Waymo’s ~1,000 vehicles in the Bay Area. I think the next important milestone is to remove the chauffeur in this region and transition toward fully autonomous rides, like in Austin.

From a price action perspective, I think the mid-term trend is holding up quite well. I see an ascending triangle pattern, which could be resolved by the end of this quarter. If shares break above the $480 level, we could see the next act of the run.

Overall, I’m resuming my Buy rating on Tesla, despite the 360x forward P/E multiple, as the autonomy story seems to hold up the share price.

data.title: Valley National Bancorp: Poised For Growth After Transformation (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Valley National Bancorp (VLY) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining 22%. The company has steadily diversified its business by reducing its commercial real estate (“CRE”) exposure and building capital to better position the company for sustainable earnings growth. Those efforts were apparent in its solid Q4 earnings. I last covered shares in September, downgrading Valley to a “Hold.” With the stock up 16%, I should have maintained my prior "Buy" rating. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit VLY.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Valley earned $0.31 per share, which beat estimates by $0.02 as revenue grew 14% to $541 million. We are seeing the benefits of a two-year repositioning that is now paying off. By growing capital, selling off some CRE loans, and focusing new lending elsewhere, VLY has greatly reduced its exposure to 333% of capital from 474% two years ago. This has put its balance sheet on a much firmer and more diversified footing. I would ideally like to see this in the 300%-325% range, but VLY has essentially completed its transformation effort.

Deposits were up over 4% from last year to $52.2 billion, with core deposits up $3.8 billion. Thanks to strong deposit growth, it was able to pay down another $500 million of high-cost brokered deposits. These indirect deposits are now just 10% of its deposit base from 14% last year. During the quarter, deposit costs fell by 124bps to 2.45%, and Valley has been able to pass on half of the rate cuts while growing deposits, a solid performance. We are also seeing improving trends in noninterest-bearing (“NIB”) balances, which are up $800 million from last year. The company’s loan-to-deposit ratio was down 140bps to 96.1% as deposit growth outpaces loan growth. I would like to see VLY operate in the 90%-95% zone, which should be achievable this year.

Loans grew $800 million sequentially to $50.1 billion. Interestingly, CRE loans grew sequentially for the first time in six quarters. Its loan pipeline is up 70% from last year, which should support ongoing loan growth. As you can see below, CRE is 40% of the loan portfolio. This is obviously still a significant exposure, and the nature of the regional banking business typically leads to a CRE-centric loan book, but the portfolio is now within more normal levels of concentration.

Digging deeper into its CRE portfolio, it has $3 billion of office exposure, but a quarter of this is owner-occupied, leaving it with ~$2.2 billion of true office risk. This portfolio is high-quality with an excellent 1.9x debt-service coverage ratio and just a 63% loan-to-value. Plus, it is clear that employers are moving away from remote working, especially in the NYC-area, and so the worst-case scenarios for office occupancy are very unlikely to occur. I do not see office being the source of widespread losses that we once feared.

Rather, the multifamily portfolio remains my biggest concern at VLY, which is unique as I generally view fundamentals here as sound (from a lender’s perspective). It has an $8.2 billion portfolio, and $2.4 billion of this sits in New York City. As previously discussed, legislative changes have impaired rent-regulated units in NY, and the company has about $1.3 billion of exposure to buildings that are majority rent-regulated. While loan-to-values are just about 65% and debt-service coverage is about 1.3x, there are likely to be some losses in the portfolio, as borrowers struggle to refinance properties. Ultimately, I view the total risk as being in the $50-$100 million over multiple years. Given credit provisions of ~$110 million per year, this can be absorbed. I view this exposure as a clear risk but a manageable one for Valley.

Net interest income (“NII”) was up 10% to $466 million. Valley’s net interest margin (“NIM”) expanded to 3.17%, up 12bps sequentially and 25bps from last year. Fixed reinvestment should be an ongoing tailwind for NIM. VLY has $1.8 billion of loans maturing this year at 4.7% in 2026, which it can deploy over 6%. Additionally, it has $4.2 billion of CDs maturing in Q1 at 3.96%, which it should be able to refinance at least 30bps lower. Given these tailwinds, I expect NIM to gradually expand to 3.27-3.35% by the end of 2026.

Valley’s effort to clean up its loan book is also paying off, with charge-offs down to 18bps in Q4. Over the past two years, it has charged off about 62bps of its CRE loans, and the pace of charge-offs is improving as it has worked through the most problematic loans. After taking $20 million of provisions in Q4, allowances for losses are 1.19% of loans, up 2bps from last year. This is 1.37x of nonaccrual loans, which is a bit below the 1.5x that I like to see. I expect the company to slightly grow reserves next year as a result.

Valley has a 10.99% tier 1 common equity capital ratio (CET1), up 17bps from last year. I view ~10.5% as an appropriate capital position for the bank (I like to see a bit higher capital given lower reserves). Still, this provides VLY with excess capital that it can use for balance sheet growth and capital returns. Indeed, with an improved capital position, Valley bought back 4 million shares in Q4. Shares also offer a 3.5% dividend yield. Depending on loan growth, I see $150-$250 million of buyback capacity during 2026.

Looking forward to 2026, Valley expects to grow loans mid-single-digits, led by strong business lending and a return to growth in CRE. Management aims to grow deposits faster than loans to enhance liquidity. This should drive an 11% increase in NII, assuming two rate cuts (given its deposit sensitivity, VLY expects NII would actually be slightly lower if the Fed cuts less, a unique position among banks). Charge-offs should continue to improve as VLY has worked through problem loans, and I expect the company to maintain a similar level of reserves, given my view a recession is unlikely.

I expect industrywide loan growth trends to improve in 2026 as lower rates and less macro uncertainty increase borrowing demand. As such, I view VLY’s target here as credible, especially since it no longer needs to work down CRE loans. Similarly, VLY has grown deposits over 4% while aggressively working down brokered balances, so growing at 5+% is consistent with core deposit trends. I expect credit provisions to be in the $105-$115 million zone to reflect some of my concern about ultimate losses in its multifamily portfolio.

I see Valley earning $1.17-$1.29 in 2026 given this outlook. That leaves shares around 10x earnings. Frankly, with many of the regional banks now trading closer to 12x, this valuation is not expensive, especially as the bank has cleaned up its balance sheet. While there remains some multifamily risk, its CRE portfolio is much cleaner, and deposit performance is very encouraging. In hindsight, my hold rating was too cautious given these fundamentals. Sometimes, it is better to allow winners to keep running, especially as the bank can shift more clearly to a growth posture.

As such, I am correcting my excess caution and reinstating a “Buy” rating. At 12x earnings, shares could approach $14.5-$15. Even removing $0.30 for potential rent-regulated losses and light reserves, fair value is over $14, over 10% above current levels. As such, I view VLY as attractive and expect continued outperformance in 2026.

data.title: Pinterest's Business Model Doesn't Fit Typical Social Media Valuations
data.description:
Introduction

Pinterest, Inc. (PINS) is still being compared to social media platforms like Meta (META) and Reddit (RDDT), but that view breaks down once you look at how the business actually makes money. Most social platforms monetize time spent, interaction, and engagement. Pinterest users, however, typically use the website with a specific planning or purchasing goal, and advertising is built around monetizing that intent. Analyzing Pinterest as another engagement-driven network leads to a misunderstanding of both profitability and valuation.

Operating Metrics Have Improved, But The Earnings Base Is Still Shallow

For much of its public history, Pinterest either operated at a loss or near breakeven. Recent quarters, however, have seen a significant improvement in both margins and revenue. The last four quarters have seen mid double-digit margins against a backdrop of steadily increasing revenue. Further cost-cutting measures are planned, as the company looks to downsize its workforce by around 15%. According to Evercore, this could save some $175 million per year.

At the same time, the company has little international revenue and the monetization base is therefore relatively narrow. In the quarter ending September 2025, Rest of World made up only 6.7% of revenue. Europe did some heavier lifting at 18.3%.

This is set to change, though. European revenue grew by 41%, and Rest of World by an impressive 66%. At the same time, Average Revenue Per User is increasingly rapidly in these regions. European ARPU rose by 31% and Rest of World was up by 44%. With MAU growth of 8% and 16% respectively, this is a clear indication that the company is doing better at monetizing existing users.

Margin Expansion Reflects Operating Leverage, Not Just Cost Cuts

Pinterest’s recent margin expansion is often dismissed as cost-driven, but that understates the role of operating leverage in the model. As advertising tools have improved and monetization efficiency has increased, higher ARPU translated into stronger incremental margins. This dynamic is consistent with an intent-driven platform, where ad relevance improves pricing power without requiring higher engagement intensity.

This also ties monetization more closely to predictable commercial cycles such as seasonal shopping, home improvement, fashion, or travel planning. Pricing power comes from context and relevance, not from how long a user stays on the platform in a given session or even how much they interact with other users.

Pinterest’s model is narrower than that of Meta or Reddit, but it’s more predictable in the sense that it monetizes specific user intent rather than broad social activity. That does not guarantee faster growth, but it does reduce reliance on daily engagement metrics, which helps explain why revenue durability matters more than raw user expansion.

Why Pinterest Looks Cheap And Expensive At The Same Time

The stock appears inexpensive on earnings-based metrics and fair on revenue-based measures as the market waits for proof of durability. The stock looks exceptionally cheap on TTM GAAP earnings especially. It trades at around 7.8 times TTM earnings, compared to Reddit’s massive 108x and Meta’s 30x. The sector median is at around 17.4x. Pinterest is also not expensive on revenue multiples relative to peers. TTM Price to Sales is only 3.8x compared to Reddit’s 18.5 and Meta’s 8.9, with price to book painting a similar picture.

A comparison to the Seeking Alpha sector median paints a different picture. The 41.8x TTM EV/EBITDA in particular is way above the average of around 9.8, and so is the FWD GAAP P/E at 35x versus 18x. The valuation discount is thus concentrated almost entirely in how the market capitalizes its newly improved margins, in my view

Earnings appear cheap because margins have only recently moved higher, while EV/EBITDA looks elevated due to the lack of EBITDA history. Forward multiples, meanwhile, suggest a level of normalization that the market is not yet willing to underwrite.

The valuation also depends on which companies one is comparing it to. The broader sector median is less useful as a benchmark because the company has a relatively unique advertising model. The sector median is composed of engagement platforms, ad-tech, and marketplaces, whereas Pinterest does not fit cleanly in any of these categories.

What The Market Still Needs To See

The market is very much taking a wait-and-see approach to Pinterest’s recent profitability. In order for the valuation discount to close, the market needs evidence that the company’s earnings base is sustainable, and led by revenue growth. Most importantly, the market wants to see that earnings can hold up during a downturn, proving the model is resistant to weaker advertising demand.

None of this requires accelerated growth, but rather persistence. If revenue continues to grow steadily while margins remain structurally positive across a weaker cycle, current earnings will become more capitalizable. Until then, the market is likely to value Pinterest conservatively, not because the business model is in doubt, but because its improved economics are still young.

Conclusion

Pinterest is not being discounted because its business model is misunderstood. The platform is already valued reasonably on revenue, reflecting the market’s recognition of its positioning and revenue durability. The remaining skepticism is concentrated in how aggressively investors are willing to capitalize a relatively new earnings base. If margin stability and international monetization continue to develop as current trends suggest, the valuation framework applied to Pinterest is likely to shift.

data.title: Apple: Booming iPhone 17 Vs. Soaring Memory Costs (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Performance Assessment

Apple Inc. (AAPL) has outperformed the broader market index since my last note on the stock:

Thesis

Apple released its Q1 FY26 (Dec '25) results yesterday. Overall, my view is balanced, as I like some but not all aspects:

  1. iPhone 17 demand is strong, and the outlook is encouraging
  2. Rising memory costs can weigh down on gross margins
  3. AAPL may be close to fair value
  4. AAPL's relative performance is stuck in a range.

iPhone 17 Demand is Strong, and the Outlook is Generally Encouraging

Apple's iPhone 17 launch in Sep '25 has so far been a big success, as it is finally leading to growth in what were rather stagnant iPhone revenues for many years now:

The highest-end Pro Max model for the iPhone 17 is doing particularly well, as the first few months of adoption rate data show record-high adoption rates for this 17-series model compared to other series:

From a geographic perspective, China (17.8% of overall revenues as of Q1 FY26) saw a very noticeable growth acceleration bump:

This was due to a record number of customers upgrading their iPhone models, but also, more encouragingly, growth from people who switched from other phone brands. Moreover, a lot of the Chinese customers are entering the broader Apple products ecosystem for the first time:

Greater China also grew 38% year-over-year, driven by iPhone, which had record upgraders and double-digit growth on switchers... We do see on non-iPhone products that the majority of customers that are buying a Mac, an iPad, a watch are still new to that product.

- CEO Timothy Cook in the Q1 FY26 earnings call.

I view this as a very encouraging sign of market share growth in China, both in terms of current sales and longer-term lifetime value [LTV] capture.

Rising Memory Costs Can Weigh Down on Gross Margins

I have discussed the memory chip price hike phenomenon in my recent articles on Micron (MU), ASML (ASML), and Nvidia (NVDA). I suggest reading those pieces to get broader context on this critical trend in the semiconductor and consumer electronics industry.

So far, these higher memory prices have not hit the financials yet. But Apple's management is warning us of an impact in Q2 FY26 (March '26 quarter) and beyond:

From a memory point of view, to answer your question, memory had a minimal impact on the Q1, so the December quarter gross margin. We do expect it to be a bit more of an impact to the Q2 gross margin and that was comprehended in the outlook of 48% to 49% that Kevan gave earlier.

Beyond Q2, we don't obviously provide outlooks beyond the current quarter. But we do continue to see market pricing for memory increasing significantly.

- CEO Timothy Cook in the Q1 FY26 earnings call.

Hence, there is a very real risk that the product gross margins may drop back down toward the mid-30s range or even lower, depending on the extent of memory price surges and Apple's exposure to that:

That said, I think the chances of product gross margin deterioration below the mid-30s may be low because there is an offsetting factor from a favorable mix-shift toward the higher-end of premium 'Pro' models in the latest iPhone product cycle:

I think given the strong iPhone cycle we're seeing, that was, I would say, probably a higher favorability than you might have seen in maybe other cycles. And as well, as you know, in Q1, typically, we do see the impact of the cost structure of our new products that we launch. And in this case, we are seeing a more favorable offset from mix of products

- CFO Kevan Parekh in the Q1 FY26 earnings call.

This is more clearly seen when we look at the various iPhone Series models' adoption curves. Notice how the most premium models are seeing a much higher rate of adoption in the chart below:

AAPL May Be Close to Fair Value

AAPL stock is current at a 1-yr fwd P/E of 30.0x, which is 18.4% higher than the longer-term average multiple of 25.3x:

Relative to some comps, AAPL is commanding a 91.3% premium to the median multiple of 15.7x:

This premium may look high but is not out of the ordinary for AAPL. In fact, the degree of the premium is actually a bit smaller than the 108% premium that AAPL stock has typically traded at in recent years:

These mixed signals (suggesting overvaluation relative to its own trading history but relative undervaluation relative to its comps) make it difficult to have a strong over or undervalued opinion on the stock. Hence, I am inclined to take the middle ground and say AAPL is probably close to fair value.

AAPL's Relative Performance is Stuck in a Range

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return, as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of AAPL vs. SPX500

AAPL has been in an outperforming uptrend over the last few years, but the momentum has gradually slowed down. Since 2022, the ratio of prices vs. the S&P 500 has been stuck in a big range. I see no net directional bias right now in my outlook.

Takeaway & Positioning

I have been disappointed by Apple's lack of iPhone sales growth in recent years. But the company has finally given us tangible signs of growth here with a successful launch of the iPhone 17 Series. The most noteworthy thing in my view is that this product launch seems to be getting a lot of traction in China, where old customers are upgrading in record numbers and new customers are joining the Apple ecosystem after leaving other brands. This is very encouraging evidence of market share gains.

From a model perspective, the most premium "Pro" models are gaining traction the most vs. other iPhone 17 models and vs. older series of the same model too. I expect the greater adoption mix coming from these models to help cushion some of the gross margin hits arising from the sharply rising memory chip prices this year.

On valuations, AAPL stock is trading above its longer term 1-yr fwd P/E range, but at a slightly lower premium than what it has typically commanded over its comps. So I think on balance, it is around fair value right now. This view is supported by the technicals, which show the stock stuck in the middle of a big range vs. the S&P 500.

Rating: Market Perform/Hold.

How to Interpret Hunting Alpha's Ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in line with the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence.

The typical time horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.
